      type: "note",
      children: _jsxs(_components.p, {
        children: ["For ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
          children: "distance"
        }), "-based comparisons, sorting should be in ascending order (smallest first), while for ", _jsx(_components.strong, {

        }), "-based algorithms, the sorting should be in descending order (largest first)."]

    }), "\n", _jsx(_components.p, {
      children: "If we inspect the results, we can see that the documents returned are ordered by relevance, with the most similar document at the top:"
    }), "\n", _jsx("center", {
      children: _jsx("img", {
        src: "../files/vector-database-result.png",
        alt: "Vector Database Result"
      })
    }), "\n", _jsx(_components.admonition, {
      type: "note",
      children: _jsxs(_components.p, {
        children: ["This demo page can be ", _jsx(_components.a, {
          href: "https://pubkey.github.io/javascript-vector-database/",
          children: "run online here"
        }), "."]
      })
    }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.p, {
      children: ["However our full-scan method presents a significant challenge: it does not scale well. As the number of stored documents increases, the time taken to fetch and compare embeddings grows proportionally. For example, retrieving embeddings from our ", _jsx(_components.a, {
        href: "https://huggingface.co/datasets/Supabase/wikipedia-en-embeddings",
        children: "test dataset"
      }), " of 10k documents takes around ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "700 milliseconds"
      }), ". If we scale up to 100k documents, this delay would rise to approximately ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "7 seconds"
      }), ", making the search process inefficient for larger datasets."]
    }), "\n", _jsx(_components.h2, {
      id: "indexing-the-embeddings-for-better-performance",
      children: "Indexing the Embeddings for Better Performance"
    }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.p, {
      children: ["To address the scalability issue, we need to store embeddings in a way that allows us to avoid fetching all of them from storage during a query. In traditional databases, you can sort documents by an ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "index field"
      }), ", allowing efficient queries that retrieve only the necessary documents. An index organizes data in a structured, sortable manner, much ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "like a phone book"
      }), ". However, with vector embeddings we are not dealing with simple, single values. Instead, we have large ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "lists of numbers"
      }), ", which makes indexing more complex because we have more than one dimension."]
    }), "\n", _jsx(_components.h3, {
      id: "vector-indexing-methods",
      children: "Vector Indexing Methods"
    }), "\n", _jsx(_components.p, {
      children: "Various methods exist for indexing these vectors to improve query efficiency and performance:"
    }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.ul, {
      children: ["\n", _jsxs(_components.li, {
        children: [_jsx(_components.a, {
          href: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Arni-zkqMBA",
          children: "Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH)"
        }), ": LSH hashes data so that similar items are likely to fall into the same bucket, optimizing approximate nearest neighbor searches in high-dimensional spaces by reducing the number of comparisons."]
      }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.li, {
        children: [_jsx(_components.a, {
          href: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=77QH0Y2PYKg",
          children: "Hierarchical Small World"
        }), ": HSW is a graph structure designed for efficient navigation, allowing quick jumps across the graph while maintaining short paths between nodes, forming the basis for HNSW's optimization."]
      }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.li, {
        children: [_jsx(_components.a, {
          href: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=77QH0Y2PYKg",
          children: "Hierarchical Navigable Small Worlds (HNSW)"
        }), ": HNSW builds a hierarchical graph for fast approximate nearest neighbor search. It uses multiple layers where higher layers represent fewer, more connected nodes, improving search efficiency in large datasets​."]
      }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.li, {
        children: [_jsx(_components.strong, {
          children: "Distance to samples"
        }), ": While testing different indexing strategies, ", _jsx(_components.a, {
          href: "https://github.com/pubkey",
          children: "I"
        }), " found out that using the distance to a sample set of items is a good way to index embeddings. You pick like 5 random items of your data and get the embeddings for them out of the model. These are your 5 index vectors. For each embedding stored in the vector database, we calculate the distance to our 5 index vectors and store that ", _jsx(_components.code, {
          children: "number"
        }), " as an index value. This seems to work good because similar things have similar distances to other things. For example the words \"shoe\" and \"socks\" have a similar distance to \"boat\" and therefore should have roughly the same index value."]
      }), "\n"]
    }), "\n", _jsxs(_components.p, {
      children: ["When building ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "local-first"
      }), " applications, performance is often a challenge, especially in JavaScript. With ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "IndexedDB"
      }), ", certain operations, like many sequential ", _jsx(_components.code, {
        children: "get by id"
      }), " calls, ", _jsx(_components.a, {
        href: "/slow-indexeddb.html",
        children: "are slow"
      }), ", while bulk operations, such as ", _jsx(_components.code, {
        children: "get by index range"
      }), ", are fast. Therefore, it's essential to use an indexing method that allows embeddings to be stored in a sortable way, like ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "Locality Sensitive Hashing"
      }), " or ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "Distance to Samples"
      }), ". In this article, we'll use ", _jsx(_components.strong, {
        children: "Distance to Samples"
      }), ", because for ", _jsx(_components.a, {
        href: "https://github.com/pubkey",
        children: "me"
      }), " it provides the best default behavior for the sample dataset."]
    }), "\n", _jsx(_components.h3, {
      id: "storing-indexed-embeddings-in-rxdb",
      children: "Storing indexed embeddings in RxDB"
